3: Dagon falls on his face before the Ark: wayyipōl dāgôn lᵉpānāyw ʾarṣāh — 'Dagon fell on his face to the ground' in the classic prostration posture of a vassal before a superior. Dāgôn is the chief deity of the Philistine cities, attested in texts from Ugarit (as Baal's father), Mari, and the Tell Dan stele context. The name is disputed — possibly from dāg (fish) or dāgān (grain). The prostration of the Philistine deity before YHWH's Ark is the text's argument: YHWH's sovereign presence does not require Israelite compliance to dominate rival deities; even in captivity, the covenant God of Israel reduces the most powerful Philistine god to prostration. The theme recurs in Acts 5 (Ananias and Sapphira falling before the Spirit's judgment) and Philippians 2:10 (every knee bowing at the name of Jesus).
4: The second morning: Dagon's head and both hands are severed and lie on the threshold — only dāgôn dāgôn, his torso, remains. The amputation of head and hands is the fate of defeated warriors (cf. 1 Samuel 17:51 — David cuts off Goliath's head; 1 Samuel 31:9 — the Philistines cut off Saul's head). Dagon is thus treated as a defeated enemy-soldier, his power symbolically neutralized. The word miptān (threshold) marks the place as the boundary where the Dagon cult's authority ended; the narrator notes that the Philistine priests 'do not tread on the threshold of Dagon in Ashdod to this day' — a surviving cultic taboo preserving the memory of this defeat embedded in Philistine practice itself.
6: The yāḏ YHWH — 'hand of YHWH' — is the theological category for direct divine action. The phrase appears six times in the Ark Narrative (1 Samuel 5:6, 7, 9, 11; 1 Samuel 6:3, 5), transforming the entire Philistine ordeal into a demonstration of YHWH's sovereign intervention without Israelite agency. Yāḏ YHWH is the Exodus vocabulary: the ten plagues are the work of YHWH's 'mighty hand and outstretched arm' (Deuteronomy 26:8). The Philistine ordeal is a mini-Exodus in reverse — YHWH operates in Philistine territory with the same plagues-and-forced-release pattern: tumors and mice parallel the Exodus plagues; the gold offerings parallel the despoliation of Egypt; the Ark's return on a cart parallels Israel's departure from Egypt. The Philistine priests make this explicit in 1 Samuel 6:6: 'Why should you harden your hearts as the Egyptians and Pharaoh hardened their hearts?'
1: The Philistines brought (vayyiqchu) the ark from Ebenezer to Ashdod. Ashdod was the most important of the five Philistine cities (Gaza, Gath, Ashkelon, Ekron, Ashdod), located on the coastal plain approximately 50 km south of Joppa. The Philistines appear to have had a central sanctuary there for Dagon. The movement of captured enemy cult objects to the victor’s temple was standard ANE practice: the Marduk statue was famously taken by the Hittites and later by the Babylonians; the Assyrians catalogued divine statues among their war trophies. The Philistines are following standard protocol.
2: The Philistines placed (vayatzigu) the ark beside Dagon (etzel Dagon). The verb natzag (hiphil: set up, place upright) is used for statues being set up. Dagon is a grain deity known from Ugaritic texts and widely worshiped in Canaan and Philistia (Judges 16:23: Samson is brought to Dagon’s temple in Gaza). The placement beside Dagon implies acknowledgment of YHWH as a subordinate deity — a trophy god next to the main god. The ark is being treated as the divine equivalent of a captured general’s standard.
5: The threshold-avoidance etiology: al-ken lo yidrekhu kohanei Dagon ve-khol ha-ba’im el-beit Dagon al-miphtan Dagon be-Ashdod ad ha-yom hazeh. The miphtan (threshold) of Dagon’s house became a no-step zone because the head and hands of Dagon had fallen there. Threshold theology is well attested in the ANE: thresholds were liminal spaces associated with protective spirits (lamassu in Mesopotamia). The etiology uses the standard formula al-ken...ad ha-yom hazeh (“therefore...to this day”) to connect ancient event to current practice — the same formula in Joshua 4:9; 1 Samuel 7:26; 1 Samuel 8:28, etc.
7: Vayir’u anshe-Ashdod ki-chen (“when the men of Ashdod saw what was happening” — literally “that it was so”). The pattern of divine plagues driving the Philistines to transfer the ark echoes the Exodus plagues that drove the Egyptians to release Israel. Philistine counsel to send away the ark mirrors Pharaoh’s successive capitulations. The phrase lo yeshev aron elohei Yisra’el ittanu (“the ark of the God of Israel must not stay with us”) acknowledges that the continuing presence of YHWH’s ark is incompatible with Philistine wellbeing — a remarkable admission.
8: The five lords of the Philistines (sarnei Pelishtim) are called to deliberate. Seren (lord/ruler of a Philistine city) is a technical term that appears only in 1 Samuel and Judges in the Hebrew Bible; it may be cognate with Greek tyrannos (tyrant/ruler) or with a Philistine root. The term consistently designates the political leadership of the pentapolis (Ashdod, Gaza, Ashkelon, Gath, Ekron). The deliberative process — gathering all the lords to decide what to do with the ark — reflects the confederacy structure of Philistine governance.
9: Yad YHWH (“the hand of the LORD”) is the primary divine action idiom across the Exodus narrative and is used consistently for divine judgment in 1 Samuel (1 Samuel 5:6, 7, 9, 11; 1 Samuel 7:13). The phrase mehumah gedolah me’od (“a very great panic/confusion”) — mehumah is divine-warrior panic terminology; YHWH sends mehumah on enemies (Deuteronomy 7:23; 1 Samuel 7:10; 1 Samuel 14:20). The tumors (Hebrew ophalim, elsewhere techorim — the two terms are used interchangeably; some manuscripts read one for the other) may correspond to bubonic plague symptoms: swollen lymph nodes (buboes). The Septuagint adds a reference to mice at this point, filling in the narrative gap.
10: Transfer to Ekron: vayishlecho et-aron ha-elohim Eqron. Ekron was the northernmost Philistine city, closest to Israelite territory. The Ekronites’ outcry — hesevu et-aron elohei Yisra’el eilai lahamiteni ve-et-ammi (“They have brought the ark of the God of Israel to us to kill us and our people”) — recognizes that the ark’s presence is lethal. The progression Ashdod → Gath → Ekron traces the arc of Philistine decision-making and the unstoppable force of divine judgment driving them toward return of the ark.
11: The Ekronites sent and gathered all the lords of the Philistines: shallechu et-aron elohei Yisra’el ve-yashuv limqomo (“Send away the ark of the God of Israel, and let it return to its own place”). Lashuv limqomo (return to its place) — the vocabulary of return to proper location is used in the priestly legislation for objects returning to their proper owners (Leviticus 25:10: ve-shavtem ish el-achuzzato, each returning to his possession). The ark has a proper place and belongs there; the Philistines can only hold it temporarily. Mehumah-mawet (“deadly panic”) is an intensified compound: the panic that causes/leads to death.
12: Those who did not die were struck with tumors (vayukku ba-ophalim). Vata’al shav’at ha-ir ha-shamayim (“and the cry of the city went up to heaven”). The phrase ta’alah shav’atam (their cry going up to heaven) echoes the cry of the Israelites in Egypt (Exodus 2:23: vata’al shav’atam el ha-elohim) — but here it is the cry of YHWH’s enemies under his judgment rather than YHWH’s people under oppression. The Ark Narrative uses the Exodus vocabulary to describe a reverse-Exodus: YHWH fighting against the oppressor from within the oppressor’s territory.
